Preface
=======

"You don't educate people by telling them useful things; you educate people by telling them interesting things." (John Conway) [Check the quote!]

## Some editorial considerations:

Birth and death dates are given as years only, to avoid confusion between Gregorian and Julian calendars — an issue especially relevant for figures from the former Russian Empire.
The symbol ∈ is used to denote disciplinary inclusion — e.g., an eponym “belonging to” a particular field of statistical theory or applied methodology.

## 🧭 Categorization of eponyms

EpoDict spans Biostatistics, Epidemiology, and related computational disciplines. Rather than assigning disciplinary labels, each eponym is categorized by its primary methodological function, allowing consistent classification across overlapping scientific fields. The list of categories is curated and may expand cautiously as the dictionary grows.
To provide readers with conceptual orientation while avoiding technical overloading, each eponym in EpoDict is accompanied by a compact field-of-use label. This is expressed using the mathematical symbol ∈, indicating that the term belongs to or is primarily associated with a particular area of statistical methodology.

## Editorial Rules for EpoDict Entries:

🔸 identity (≤ 5 words)
Style: Neutral, title-case; omit "a"/"the" unless essential
Origin principle: Based on the *ethnic and modern nation-state approach*. Although it may sound anachronistic, this method provides a consistent and practical framework for categorizing historical figures in a way that is accessible to contemporary readers and avoids shift of attention and credits from national to imperial identities, thust preserving cultural diversity.
Examples:
Danish mathematician, statistician, and engineer
American epidemiologist and data analyst
British geneticist and public health pioneer
German-born biostatistician

🔸 eponym (≤ 10 words per line)
EpoDict spans Biostatistics, Epidemiology, and related computational disciplines. Rather than assigning disciplinary labels, each eponym is categorized by its primary methodological function, allowing consistent classification across overlapping scientific fields. The list of categories is curated and may expand cautiously as the dictionary grows.
Examples:
- Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel (CMH) test ∈ stratified analysis
- Daniell-Kolmogorov theorem ∈ stochastic processes
  
🔸 histor_facts (max 2–4 lines, 1–2 sentences each)
Style: Telegraphic but clear; no initial articles unless needed; past tense preferred
Examples:
Developed queueing theory to model telephone systems.
Published foundational works in statistical mechanics.
Charitable, reclusive, and known for helping students anonymously.

🔸 full_name
Style: Full historical name, with correct spelling and accents
Examples:
Karl Pearson
Agner Krarup Erlang

🔸 chapter
Style: First letter of surname (capital)

🔸 finalized

Style: TRUE [or FALSE] only

The Eponymous Dictionary is intentionally selective. It includes only those eponyms that belong explicitly and recognizably to the core domains of Statistics, Biostatistics, Epidemiology, Data Science, [Formal and Mathematical] Logic, Cybernetics, Bioinformatics, Medical Informatics, Biometry, Biomathematics, or Computational Health Sciences. Purely mathematical or purely biomedical constructs are excluded unless they have been adopted into statistical usage and are commonly encountered in professional discourse within the above fields. This editorial choice affirms the disciplinary integrity of Biostatistics and Epidemiology as distinct scientific languages—not merely subsets of Mathematics, Biology, or Medicine.
If a concept belongs to classical or applied probability theory — and is not purely philosophical or abstract — it shall be included in EpoDict, unless it belongs exclusively to pure measure theory, topology, or functional analysis without any recognized statistical modeling relevance.
In the Eponymous Dictionary, possessive or nobiliary prefix particles in complex surnames (e.g., de, d', di, von, van, etc.) are disregarded in both the entry headings and the alphabetical order of surnames in the Index. Thus, to locate a figure in the dictionary, one should search by the core surname itself (e.g., FINETTI rather than DE FINETTI; VINCI rather than DA VINCI).
To preserve conceptual clarity and avoid inflation, the Eponymous Dictionary includes only distinct theoretical results, methods, or tools that are eponymous and recognized as standalone contributions within Statistics, Biostatistics, Epidemiology, and related fields.
Derivative coinages — such as -ization, -statistic, or -estimator forms of existing eponyms — are excluded unless they have acquired independent theoretical status. This ensures the dictionary remains focused, didactic, and non-redundant.

All logical and set-theoretic symbols used throughout the dictionary follow the Anglo-American standard, directly compatible with the Julia programming environment. For detailed correspondences, see Appendix.


##NB! Images

Unless otherwise noted, the portraits and microphotographs in this dictionary are used under Public Domain or Creative Commons Zero (PD/CC0) licenses. We gratefully acknowledge the contributors to repositories for supporting global open-access education.

## FEEDBACK FROM READERS

This is an evolving digital-publishing project, and with your help we can make this book even better.
At the end of each section of the book you’ll find a “magical” feedback link. Clicking the link takes you to an online feedback form where you can share your thoughts with us.
We welcome any and all feedback or suggestions for improvement you may have. Please feel free to be as terse or detailed as you see fit. 
All feedback is stored anonymously, but you can choose to leave your name and contact information so we can follow up or mention you on our “Thank You” page.
We use a different feedback link for each section, so we’ll always know which part of the book your notes refer to.
Thank you for helping us make this book an even more valuable reference resource for our readers community.

## Abbreviations used

Syn.: Synonym(s)
